text.title: Contextual Inquiry: Four Principles and Observation Rules
text.summary: Defines the four core principles (context, partnership, interpretation, focus) and rules for what to capture during a session.

rule.statement: Conduct the session in the user's actual work environment, not a lab or meeting room.
rule.rationale: Context principle: removing the user from their environment removes the environmental triggers, physical artifacts, and interruptions that shape real behavior.

rule.statement: Treat the user as the expert and the researcher as the apprentice (learner). Use the opening script to establish this frame before observation begins.
rule.rationale: Partnership principle: the master-apprentice model reduces observer-effect distortion. Users perform naturally when they are the acknowledged authority, not the subject under evaluation.

rule.statement: Share interpretations aloud during the session and invite corrections: "So it seems like... Did I understand correctly?"
rule.rationale: Interpretation principle: researchers form wrong mental models continuously. Surfacing and correcting them in-session prevents compounded misinterpretations in analysis.

rule.statement: Enter each session with clear research questions. The observation guide must specify which aspects of work to observe and what questions to answer.
rule.rationale: Focus principle: without a defined lens, sessions produce unfocused notes that are expensive to analyze. Clear focus enables selective attention without missing key phenomena.

rule.statement: Record these seven data types for every session: actions taken, tools used, artifacts created or used, physical environment description, interruptions, workarounds, and frustrations expressed verbally.
rule.rationale: Comprehensive capture prevents the most common loss: workarounds and physical artifacts (sticky notes, paper forms) that hold the real workflow are easy to overlook when focused only on screen interactions.

rule.statement: Mark inferences as inferences in notes from the very first session. Write observed vs inferred in separate fields; retroactive separation after analysis is impossible.
rule.rationale: Mixing observed and inferred data produces false confidence. Downstream agents and designers treat both as equally grounded unless the distinction is explicit.

example.note: Opening frame that establishes master-apprentice model without leading the user.
example.code: "I'm here to learn how you do your work. You're the expert. I'll watch and ask questions. Please do your work as normally as possible. If you explain something I don't understand, I'll ask. If I interpret something wrong, please correct me."
